hints.noun clause ('that'): 'Quia' can mean 'that' in Vulgate Latin after verbs of saying, but here it gives the reason for rejoicing
hints.relative clause: Relative clauses use 'qui/quae/quod' — 'quia' is a conjunction

steps.reveal: hic filius meus = this son of mine (nom.)

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'hunc filium meum' — 'hic filius meus' is nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'fili mi' — 'hic filius meus' is nominative subject
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'huius filii mei' — this is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'filii' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filii' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'filii' ends in -i

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'mortuus erat' translates to?

hints.to kill: 'To kill' is 'occidere' or 'necare' — 'mortuus erat' comes from 'mori' (to die)
hints.to remain: 'To remain' is 'manere' or 'morari' — 'mori' means to die

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'mortuus erat'?
steps.reveal: mortuus erat = had died / was dead

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'mortuus est' — 'erat' makes this pluperfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'moriebatur' — 'mortuus erat' is pluperfect
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: 'Mori' is deponent — passive in form but active in meaning

hints.moror, morari, moratus sum: These are the parts of 'morari' (to delay) — different deponent verb
hints.mordeo, mordere, momordi, morsum: These are the parts of 'mordere' (to bite)
hints.moveo, movere, movi, motum: These are the parts of 'movere' (to move)

steps.target: to come back to life / to revive
choices: to come back to life / to revive

hints.to see again: 'To see again' is 'revidere' — 'revixit' comes from 'reviviscere' or 'revivere'
hints.to revisit: 'To revisit' is 'revisere' — 'revivere' means to come back to life
hints.to rule again: 'To rule again' is 'regnare iterum' — 'revivere' means to revive

steps.reveal: revixit = he has come back to life / he revived

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'reviviscit' — 'revixit' with -xit is perfect
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'reviviscebat' — 'revixit' is perfect indicative

hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live) — the base verb without the re- prefix
hints.revideo, revidere, revidi, revisum: These are the parts of 'revidere' (to see again) — different verb
hints.reviso, revisere, revisi, revisum: These are the parts of 'revisere' (to revisit) — different verb

steps.prompt: What is the rhetorical force of the contrast 'mortuus erat...revixit'?
steps.target: antithesis: death vs. new life, expressing the father's joy at restoration
choices: antithesis: death vs. new life, expressing the father's joy at restoration

hints.parallelism: repeating the same idea: The two clauses express opposite ideas (dead vs. alive), not the same idea
hints.litotes: understatement: This is not understatement — 'dead and alive again' is a powerful direct antithesis
hints.hyperbole: exaggeration for effect: While metaphorical (the son did not literally die), the figure is antithesis, not hyperbole

steps.prompt: What is the tense sequence significance of 'mortuus erat' (pluperfect) vs. 'revixit' (perfect)?
steps.target: the pluperfect shows the prior state; the perfect shows the new, current reality
choices: the pluperfect shows the prior state; the perfect shows the new, current reality
both refer to the same time
the pluperfect is more recent than the perfect

hints.both refer to the same time: The pluperfect is further past than the perfect — 'was dead' preceded 'has come alive'
hints.the pluperfect is more recent than the p: The opposite: pluperfect is more remote — the son 'had been dead' before he 'came alive'
hints.the pluperfect shows repeated action: Repeated action is imperfect — pluperfect shows a completed prior state

translation: Because this son of mine was dead and has come back to life
